title: Authenticated Crawls, Sessions, Tenant Isolation, and Privacy · Narrowing Workshop
summary: A disability-led public-interest technology team incrementally builds least-authority session and privacy policy from original deterministic HTTP, robots, sitemap, HTML, browser, and report fixtures while retaining prior TypeScript, Node, web, Git, accessibility, security, testing, and recovery requirements. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of least-authority session and privacy policy while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit…
objectives: Require explicit owner and account authorization, purpose, role, environment, page scope, time window, and revocation for authenticated access.
Acquire secrets through approved channels, avoid source and logs, scope them minimally, rotate exposure, and destroy temporary material.
Permit safe retrieval only, block state-changing forms and logout or destructive routes, and test same-site side effects.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Session Authorization
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop TypeScript team handles case 9227 during a multilingual archive markup migration. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove least-authority sessions, secrets, safe methods, isolation, minimization, and derived-data deletion; release evidence binds source, compiler, emitted JavaScript, lockfile, package, browser revision, container, configuration, and restored state; then inject one authenticated, private-data, output-injection, path, log, plugin, or correction fault and contain it. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Session Authorization.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for require explicit owner and account authorization, purpose, role, environment, page scope, time window, and revocation for authenticated access. For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Session Authorization, keep Session Authorization connected to executable TypeScript authorization, URL, runtime admission, Fetch, Promise, browser, accessibility, report, package, and recovery decision evidence.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Session Authorization has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Plan the evidence sequence · Workshop Session Authorization, name the owner, authorization, source and emitted revision, Node, TypeScript, URL, robots, unknown value, Fetch, Promise, AbortSignal, browser, accessibility, report, handle, recovery, and native-transfer boundary, Session Authorization precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Plan the evidence sequence · Workshop Session Authorization, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A workshop TypeScript team handles case 4893 during an isolated browser, package, and restore smoke. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove least-authority sessions, secrets, safe methods, isolation, minimization, and derived-data deletion; release evidence binds source, compiler, emitted JavaScript, lockfile, package, browser revision, container, configuration, and restored state; then inject one authenticated, private-data, output-injection, path, log, plugin, or correction fault and contain it. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Require explicit owner and account authorization, purpose, role, environment, page scope, time window, and revocation for authenticated access.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Plan the evidence sequence · Workshop Session Authorization, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Valid credentials authorize every action available to the account.

options.text: Declare Authorized site auditing and TypeScript method semantics, target authority, credentials, body ownership, and byte and time budgets for the workshop Session Authorization case

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Sensitive Data Co…
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-TypeScript function headed "// Evidence: crawler-ts-sensitive-data-controls-mdv6jl" that uses case 6622 to prove least-authority sessions, secrets, safe methods, isolation, minimization, and derived-data deletion. Keep defaults runnable and return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import Node APIs, open streams or sockets, resolve DNS, parse through Cheerio, launch Playwright or axe-core, access files or packages, retain credentials, leave handles, or claim native runtime, accessibility, privacy, legal, load, container, restore, or production behavior; verify those boundaries later with Node 24.18.0, TypeScript 7.0.2 and the TypeScript 6.0.2 compatibility compiler API, Cheerio 1.2.0, robots-parser 3.0.1, p-limit 7.3.0, csv-stringify 6.8.1, Playwright 1.61.1, axe-core 4.12.1, original fixtures, loopback servers, emitted-runtime and handle tests, controlled DNS and browser environments, accessibility review, packages, containers, capacity, backup, restore, rollback, legal review, and release gates.

Evidence target: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Sensitive Data Co….
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Sensitive Data Controls into observable performance evidence. For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Sensitive Data Co…, keep Sensitive Data Controls connected to executable TypeScript authorization, URL, runtime admission, Fetch, Promise, browser, accessibility, report, package, and recovery decision evidence.

content.text: The Sensitive Data Controls structural check is only one gate. least-authority session and privacy policy must also survive the stated changed case and causal explanation.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Sensitive Data Contr…
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop TypeScript team handles case 4179 during an isolated browser, package, and restore smoke. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove least-authority sessions, secrets, safe methods, isolation, minimization, and derived-data deletion; every external value remains unknown until runtime admission produces an owned value; then change one duplicate, accessibility state, axe result, resource budget, browser readiness, or manual-review condition without overclaiming. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Minimize, redact, encrypt where required, restrict access, expire, delete, and audit sensitive content through every derived artifact.

One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?

stimulus.title: Sensitive Data Controls failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Redacting final CSV removes sensitive values from all intermediate state.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Minimize, redact, encrypt where required, restrict access, expire, delete, and audit sensitive content through every derived artifact.

options.text: Keep the plausible Sensitive Data Controls output and hide the violated least-authority session and privacy policy assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for Sensitive Data Controls.

For test an acceptance boundary, fetch one convenient page, omit owner authorization, source and emitted revision, URL and robots policy, runtime admission, stream and Promise ownership, Cheerio and browser differences, accessibility states, changed fixtures, recovery, and controlled Node transfer, then accept plausible objects as TypeScript crawler proof. This does not satisfy Sensitive Data Controls for least-authority session and privacy policy.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Sensitive Data Controls condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Minimize, redact, encrypt where required, restrict access, expire, delete, and audit sensitive content through every derived artifact.
